case_name: K.G. Srinivasamurthy vs Habib Khathun And Ors.
compensation_amount: Suma (minor): Rs. 3,50,000/-; Habib Khathun (mother of deceased Inthu Pasha): Rs. 1,95,000/-

compensation_breakdown.interest: 9% p.a. from date of petition to date of payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: Out of the compensation Rs. 3,10,000 shall be deposited in the name of the minor for five years; guardian may withdraw interest for upkeep.

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Habib Khathun (mother of deceased Inthu Pasha)
compensation_breakdown.interest: 9% p.a. from date of petition to date of payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: Out of the compensation Rs. 1,40,000 shall be deposited in the name of the claimant for five years and Rs. 55,000 to be disbursed to the claimant.

facts: This judgment disposes of two connected MACT claims: M.V.C. No. 177 of 1997 (Suma, a minor aged about 3-4 years, injured on 13.1.1997 when lorry No. MYT 6600 allegedly dashed against her causing crush injury and amputation of right lower limb above knee) and M.V.C. No. 140 of 1994 (death of Inthu Pasha on 19.1.1994 as pillion rider when struck by Canter lorry No. KA 18-240). Claim petitions sought Rs.10,00,000 and Rs.5,00,000 respectively against owners and insurers. Tribunals found rash and negligent driving in both cases, awarded Rs.3,10,000 (injury) and Rs.1,64,000 (death) respectively and split positions on insurer liability based on drivers' licence status.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued they suffered severe losses (amputation and death) due to rash and negligent driving and sought full damages; the claimant in M.V.C. No.177 sought enhancement of the Tribunal award given permanent disability. They relied on statutory third‑party protection under Chapter XI of the Motor Vehicles Act and on precedents holding insurers liable to third parties. Claimant in M.V.C. No.140 sought enhancement of dependency award and contended insurer should be made liable where appropriate.
arguments_respondent: The insurers contended they were not liable because the drivers did not hold an effective/valid driving licence on the dates of the accidents (licences had expired and were renewed only after the accidents), thus breaching policy conditions and excluding insurer liability. Owners/respondents argued quantum was excessive and, in some respects, accepted insurer liability but sought reduction of amounts. Insurers urged that policy exclusion clauses and licence non‑renewal absolved them from indemnity to the insured.
ratio_decidendi: "In view of the above said decisions of the Apex Court, it is clear that the liability of the insurance company to satisfy the award passed against third party is a statutory liability and the same is not inhibited by any of the conditions imposed in the policy and any breach of conditions imposed in the policy would not bind the third party and the insurance company is bound to satisfy the award against the third party; and if the insurance company is able to prove that there was breach of conditions of the policy vis-a-vis the insured and can avoid liability under the policy, it can recover from the insured, the amount paid by it to the third party by virtue of its statutory liability under the Motor Vehicles Act." "The effect of the above provisions is this: When a valid insurance policy has been issued in respect of a vehicle as evidenced by a certificate of insurance the burden is on the insurer to pay to third parties, whether or not there has been any breach or violation of the policy conditions." "But the amount so paid by the insurer to third parties can be allowed to be recovered from the insured if as per the policy conditions the insurer had no liability to pay such sum to the insured." "Where the breach alleged is not a factor which has contributed to the causing of the accident, the said breach would not be a fundamental breach so as to afford a ground to the insurer to avoid liability altogether and the exclusion clause must be read down so as to serve the main purpose of the policy, i.e., to indemnify the insured."
final_order: M.F.A. No. 5566 of 1999 dismissed; M.F.A. No. 690 of 2000 allowed in part and cross‑objection allowed in part: claimant in M.V.C. No.177 awarded Rs.3,50,000 with interest at 9% p.a. from date of petition (Rs.3,10,000 to be deposited in name of minor for five years); M.F.A. No.3557 of 1998 allowed and cross‑objection allowed in part: claimant in M.V.C. No.140 awarded Rs.1,95,000 with interest at 9% p.a., insurer jointly and severally liable with owner (Rs.1,40,000 to be deposited for five years, Rs.55,000 disbursed).
summary: This High Court judgment (Karnataka, 19 Oct 2001) deals with two motor accident claims: a severe injury/amputation of a child (M.V.C.177/1997) and a fatality (M.V.C.140/1994). The court held rash and negligent driving caused both accidents, enhanced Tribunal awards to Rs.3,50,000 and Rs.1,95,000 respectively, and held insurers jointly and severally liable despite licence‑renewal lapses because mere delay in renewal did not establish a wilful breach that would defeat third‑party entitlement. The court applied the statutory principle that insurers must satisfy third‑party awards but may recover from insureds on proof of fundamental breach; interest was fixed at 9% p.a.
embedding_summary: K.G. Srinivasamurthy v. Habib Khathun & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, 19 Oct 2001 (I(2002)ACC557 etc.). Two MACT matters: M.V.C. No.177/1997 (Suma injured on 13.1.1997 by lorry MYT 6600; right lower limb amputated) and M.V.C. No.140/1994 (Inthu Pasha killed on 19.1.1994 by Canter lorry KA 18-240). Key statutes: Chapter XI, Sections 145–164, Section 147, Section 149(2)(a)(ii) and 149(4), Section 15 MV Act 1988; doctrines: insurer statutory liability to third parties, "pay-and-recover" (insurer may recover from insured on proof of breach), burden on insurer to prove wilful breach, reading down exclusion clauses. Court increased awards to Rs.3,50,000 (injury) and Rs.1,95,000 (death) with interest at 9% p.a., held insurers jointly and severally liable, and ordered specified deposits for minors/claimants (Rs.3,10,000 and Rs.1,40,000).
legal_principles: Statutory insurer liability to third parties: insurer must satisfy awards despite policy exclusions
Pay‑and‑recover doctrine: insurer may recover from insured on proof of breach of policy conditions
Burden on insurer to prove wilful breach of policy conditions to escape liability
Delay in renewal of licence (interregnum) does not ipso facto absolve insurer unless wilful/disqualifying breach is shown
Exclusion clauses must be read down to serve the main purpose of indemnifying third‑party victims
Interest on awards to be moderated in light of prevailing rates (reduced to 9% p.a.)
cases_cited: New Asiatic Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Pessumal Dhanamal Aswani, 1958-65 ACJ 559 (SC)
Skandia Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Kokilaben Chandravadan, 1987 ACJ 411 (SC)

Kashiram Yadav v. Oriental Fire & General Ins. Co. Ltd.
bm25_text: K.G. Srinivasamurthy vs Habib Khathun And Ors. High Court of Karnataka 2001 This judgment disposes of two connected MACT claims: M.V.C. No. 177 of 1997 (Suma, a minor aged about 3-4 years, injured on 13.1.1997 when lorry No. MYT 6600 allegedly dashed against her causing crush injury and amputation of right lower limb above knee) and M.V.C. No. 140 of 1994 (death of Inthu Pasha on 19.1.1994 as pillion rider when struck by Canter lorry No. KA 18-240). Claim petitions sought Rs.10,00,000 and Rs.5,00,000 respectively against owners and insurers. Tribunals found rash and negligent driving in both cases, awarded Rs.3,10,000 (injury) and Rs.1,64,000 (death) respectively and split positions on insurer liability based on drivers' licence status. Claimants argued they suffered severe losses (amputation and death) due to rash and negligent driving and sought full damages; the claimant in M.V.C. No.177 sought enhancement of the Tribunal award given permanent disability. They relied on statutory third‑party protection under Chapter XI of the Motor Vehicles Act and on precedents holding insurers liable to third parties. Claimant in M.V.C. No.140 sought enhancement of dependency award and contended insurer should be made liable where appropriate. The insurers contended they were not liable because the drivers did not hold an effective/valid driving licence on the dates of the accidents (licences had expired and were renewed only after the accidents), thus breaching policy conditions and excluding insurer liability. Owners/respondents argued quantum was excessive and, in some respects, accepted insurer liability but sought reduction of amounts. Insurers urged that policy exclusion clauses and licence non‑renewal absolved them from indemnity to the insured. "In view of the above said decisions of the Apex Court, it is clear that the liability of the insurance company to satisfy the award passed against third party is a statutory liability and the same is not inhibited by any of the conditions imposed in the policy and any breach of conditions imposed in the policy would not bind the third party and the insurance company is bound to satisfy the award against the third party; and if the insurance company is able to prove that there was breach of conditions of the policy vis-a-vis the insured and can avoid liability under the policy, it can recover from the insured, the amount paid by it to the third party by virtue of its statutory liability under the Motor Vehicles Act." "The effect of the above provisions is this: When a valid insurance policy has been issued in respect of a vehicle as evidenced by a certificate of insurance the burden is on the insurer to pay to third parties, whether or not there has been any breach or violation of the policy conditions." "But the amount so paid by the insurer to third parties can be allowed to be recovered from the insured if as per the policy conditions the insurer had no liability to pay such sum to the insured." "Where the breach alleged is not a factor which has contributed to the causing of the accident, the said breach would not be a fundamental breach so as to afford a ground to the insurer to avoid liability altogether and the exclusion clause must be read down so as to serve the main purpose of the policy, i.e., to indemnify the insured." M.F.A. No. 5566 of 1999 dismissed; M.F.A. No. 690 of 2000 allowed in part and cross‑objection allowed in part: claimant in M.V.C. No.177 awarded Rs.3,50,000 with interest at 9% p.a. from date of petition (Rs.3,10,000 to be deposited in name of minor for five years); M.F.A. No.3557 of 1998 allowed and cross‑objection allowed in part: claimant in M.V.C. No.140 awarded Rs.1,95,000 with interest at 9% p.a., insurer jointly and severally liable with owner (Rs.1,40,000 to be deposited for five years, Rs.55,000 disbursed). This High Court judgment (Karnataka, 19 Oct 2001) deals with two motor accident claims: a severe injury/amputation of a child (M.V.C.177/1997) and a fatality (M.V.C.140/1994). The court held rash and negligent driving caused both accidents, enhanced Tribunal awards to Rs.3,50,000 and Rs.1,95,000 respectively, and held insurers jointly and severally liable despite licence‑renewal lapses because mere delay in renewal did not establish a wilful breach that would defeat third‑party entitlement. The court applied the statutory principle that insurers must satisfy third‑party awards but may recover from insureds on proof of fundamental breach; interest was fixed at 9% p.a. K.G. Srinivasamurthy v. Habib Khathun & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, 19 Oct 2001 (I(2002)ACC557 etc.). Two MACT matters: M.V.C. No.177/1997 (Suma injured on 13.1.1997 by lorry MYT 6600; right lower limb amputated) and M.V.C. No.140/1994 (Inthu Pasha killed on 19.1.1994 by Canter lorry KA 18-240). Key statutes: Chapter XI, Sections 145–164, Section 147, Section 149(2)(a)(ii) and 149(4), Section 15 MV Act 1988; doctrines: insurer statutory liability to third parties, "pay-and-recover" (insurer may recover from insured on proof of breach), burden on insurer to prove wilful breach, reading down exclusion clauses. Court increased awards to Rs.3,50,000 (injury) and Rs.1,95,000 (death) with interest at 9% p.a., held insurers jointly and severally liable, and ordered specified deposits for minors/claimants (Rs.3,10,000 and Rs.1,40,000). Suma (minor) Rs. 3,10,000/- (including interim Rs. 25,000) Rs. 3,50,000/- 9% p.a. from date of petition to date of payment Out of the compensation Rs. 3,10,000 shall be deposited in the name of the minor for five years; guardian may withdraw interest for upkeep. Habib Khathun (mother of deceased Inthu Pasha) Rs. 1,64,000/- Rs. 1,95,000/- 9% p.a. from date of petition to date of payment Out of the compensation Rs. 1,40,000 shall be deposited in the name of the claimant for five years and Rs. 55,000 to be disbursed to the claimant.
